Description: Optimal average power for Molybdenum oxide removal without substrate damage
Near-IR wavelength providing good absorption for Molybdenum surface contaminants
Beam spot diameter for effective cleaning resolution and energy density
Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage with thermal management
Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal from Molybdenum surface
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient contaminant ablation with minimal thermal diffusion
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning with adequate overlap
Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap percentage for uniform cleaning coverage

Typical range for 1064 nm pulsed lasers in metal cleaning; starting at 100 W balances efficiency and safety for Mo's high reflectivity and thermal conductivity, avoiding melting (threshold ~300 W). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and studies on refractory metals (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

Effective for contamination removal; 1500 mm/s optimal for light contaminants on Mo, slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy; ensures uniform cleaning without excessive heat buildup. Source: Laserax equipment manuals and industry standards for IR laser cleaning.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of reflective metals like Mo; 30 ns minimizes HAZ compared to longer pulses, while ps/fs (not typical at 1064 nm for industrial) reduce thermal effects further. Source: Laser physics reviews (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2020).

Standard for pulsed cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for Mo to achieve fast processing without significant heat accumulation due to its conductivity; higher rates (>50 kHz) increase speed but risk thermal damage. Source: Academic literature on pulse train effects (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2019).

Fluence for contaminant ablation without substrate damage; Mo's ablation threshold ~3-5 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to reflectivity; 1.5 J/cm² safe for cleaning. Conservative estimate from similar metals. Source: Ablation threshold studies (J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. 2017).

Beam diameter for precise industrial cleaning; 200 μm recommended for Mo surface prep, balancing resolution for fine details and coverage for larger areas. Source: Manufacturer specs (e.g., Coherent Armo series) and process optimization.

Typically 1-3 passes for complete removal on metals; 2 passes recommended for Mo to ensure thorough cleaning of oxides/contaminants without overexposure. Source: Industry case studies on refractory metal processing (Laser Cleaning Handbook, 2021).
Ensures uniform coverage; 50% optimal for Mo to avoid gaps or uneven heating, adjustable higher for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization studies (Opt. Laser Technol. 2022).
